pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Automate drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when outputs are clearly marked as AI-generated, because these are considered preparation that a human can inspect before taking action.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 explicitly approves automatic drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when clearly marked as generated and explains the preparation-vs-authority rationale. S5 also lists drafting and artifact linking as safe defaults.

claimJudgments.claimText: Require human approval for policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking because they change obligations or can stop work without accountable approval.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 rejects automatic policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking because they change obligations or stop work without accountable approval. S5 likewise says these actions require an approver recorded in the artifact trail.

claimJudgments.claimText: Automations must not present recommendations as approved decisions; use explicit status labels (e.g., draft, recommendation, approved action, blocked) to prevent teams from acting prematurely.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 directly describes an incident where a recommendation was mistaken for an approved decision, causing teams to pause rollout work, and asks for the listed status labels. S5 provides the same taxonomy, and S6 recommends visible status labels before action.

claimJudgments.claimText: Any automated message that could be interpreted as a customer commitment requires an accountable human reviewer; internal draft summaries are acceptable only if they cannot be confused with final commitments.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 explicitly states that automated messages interpretable as customer commitments need an accountable human reviewer and that internal draft summaries are approved only if not confused with final commitments.

claimJudgments.claimText: User acceptance improves when evidence, recommendation, and action controls are separated; combining recommendation and execution controls increases cancellations and clarification behavior.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly reports higher acceptance when evidence, recommendation, and action controls are separated, and more cancellations and clarification clicks when recommendation and action are clustered. S6 reinforces the UI separation guidance.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation directly answers which actions can be automated versus require approval. It is well supported by the packet: preparation work can be automated with generated-status labeling; policy changes, release blocks, customer commitments, and external messages require human approval with an approver trail; UI/status-label guardrails are supported by the incident, analytics, and interface notes. No major unsupported recommendation was found.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
